title: Tuile biscuits
subtitle: Thin, curved almond biscuits shaped over a rolling pin
excerpt: Tuiles are very thin, lacy biscuits made from a batter of butter, egg white, sugar, and a little flour, spread thinly on a baking sheet and shaped over a rolling pin while still warm and pliable from the oven. They cool into a curved, crisp wafer. The window for shaping is about 20 seconds; work fast.
sourceNotes: Tuiles (roof tiles) are a classic French patisserie preparation. The name refers to the curved roofing tiles of France. The basic formula of egg whites, sugar, butter, and flour is well documented in French pastry literature and is firmly in the public domain.

recipe.batchNotes: Bake 4 to 5 at a time; do not bake a full tray, as the shaping window is too short.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Tuiles soften quickly in humidity; make on the day of serving. Store in an airtight tin in a cool, dry place.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; bake in small batches so all biscuits come out simultaneously

content.text: Tuiles are baked from a thin batter spread into circles on a baking sheet. The batter sets into a lacy, amber biscuit in about 7 minutes and must be lifted and draped over a rolling pin immediately, before it sets into a flat sheet. Once curved and cooled, they hold their shape and stay crisp for several hours in a dry room. They suit desserts that need a crisp contrast.

Whisk the egg whites, icing sugar, and vanilla briefly until combined but not foamy. Fold in the flour, then fold in the melted butter. Stir in the flaked almonds. Refrigerate the batter for 30 minutes; chilling firms the butter and makes spreading easier.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Drop teaspoons of batter onto the tray, spacing them at least 8 cm apart, and use the back of the spoon to spread each into a thin circle, about 8 cm across. Bake 4 to 5 at a time for 7 to 8 minutes until the edges are deep gold and the centres are lightly coloured. Work to the right size: if the circles are too thick, they bake too slowly; if too thin, they burn.

Remove from the oven and leave for 30 seconds. Lift each tuile immediately with a palette knife and lay it over a rolling pin to curve. It should conform in seconds. Work fast: the window is about 20 seconds before the tuile hardens and snaps if bent. If one hardens before you shape it, return the tray to the oven for 30 seconds to soften. Slide the curved tuiles off the rolling pin onto a wire rack and leave to cool and set fully.
